sentence: Tyrannosaurus rex is one of the most famous dinosaurs ever.
For such a huge, powerful hunter, it had surprisingly tiny arms.
T-rex was about 40 feet long and could weigh as much as 9 tons.
Its arms were only about 3 feet long.
With arms that short, it couldn't even reach its own mouth.
Why would such a fierce predator have arms that seem almost useless?
Scientists have known for decades that T-rex isn't the only dinosaur like this.
Fossils from several other dinosaur families show the same pattern.
They too had tiny arms, giant bodies, and huge heads with powerful jaws.
These groups were only distantly related, yet these same features evolved independently multiple times.
What could have driven dinosaurs to separately develop small arms so many times?
To understand why, scientists measured skull sizes alongside arm sizes across many different species.
A clear pattern emerged.
Where skulls grew bigger and more robust, the arms tended to get shorter and the overall body size increased.
One idea is that as the prey animals got bigger and tougher, a powerful bite became more and more important.
Over millions of years, skulls grew larger and jaws grew more powerful.
As the skull became the main tool for catching prey, the arms were no longer needed.
Growing large arms takes energy, so gradually the arms got smaller and smaller.
Not all dinosaurs followed this path.
Plant-eating dinosaurs kept their smaller heads and longer arms.
So did dinosaurs that hunted in different ways.
T-rex's tiny arms may look strange, but they weren't a flaw.
By that point, T-rex's jaws had simply taken over the job.
The tiny arms were a consequence of the skull becoming T-rex's main weapon.
It was becoming one of the most powerful predators on Earth.
Humpback whales are known for their complex songs and exciting jumps out of the water.
They are also some of the most creative hunters in the ocean.
These whales use bubbles to trap and catch fish, in a hunting technique called bubble net feeding.
Here's how it works.
A whale dives below a school of fish.
It swims in a slow upward spiral, blowing air through its blowhole.
The bubbles rise to the surface and form a curtain.
It is like a net made of air, and the fish trapped inside can't escape.
Then the whale lunges up through the middle with its mouth open wide.
It swallows an enormous mouthful of fish in one gulp.
Some humpback whales do this alone, but others hunt in teams.
When they work together, they take on different roles.
Some whales produce the bubbles, and others herd the fish toward the center.
Some even make loud calls that may help herd the fish tighter.
Each whale has a role, and the whole team has to coordinate for the hunt to succeed.
Scientists tracked hundreds of individual humpback whales in British Columbia, Canada, over twenty years.
Humpback whales have distinctive tail markings which scientists used to tell individual whales apart.
The scientists tracked which whales spent time together.
They also noted which whales knew how to bubble net feed and when they fed alone or together.
They found strong evidence that the bubble net technique spreads through social contact.
Whales that spent more time together were far more likely to both use it.
This suggests that bubble net feeding is not something whales are born knowing or that they invent on their own.
It is a skill that is learned by watching and copying other whales.
This is similar to how you might learn how to cook by watching family members in the kitchen.
The cat sat on the mat.
The dog barked at a squirrel.
The bunny rabbit nibbled a carrot.
